metadata.summary: Cloud Run traffic management: blue-green deployments, canary rollouts, gradual traffic shifting, rollback procedures, revision tagging, and Cloud Build CI/CD integration.

content.title: Cloud Run Traffic Management
content.summary: Cloud Run supports revision-based traffic splitting: deploy a new revision without traffic, test it via a tagged URL, then gradually shift traffic using update-traffic commands. Revisions are immutable; rollback is instant via --to-revisions pointing to a prior revision. Tags give stable preview URLs independent of traffic allocation.

why.p: Cloud Run's traffic model separates deployment from release. A new revision can be deployed with --no-traffic, tested at its tagged URL (green---my-app.run.app), and only promoted to production traffic when validated. This decoupling enables blue-green, canary, and A/B patterns without downtime or redeployment. Rollback takes seconds — point 100% traffic to a prior revision, no rebuild required.

list.item: Releasing a new version of a Cloud Run service with zero-downtime.
Testing a new revision before routing production traffic to it.
Implementing canary releases with gradual traffic percentages.
Rolling back a bad deployment to a prior revision instantly.
Running A/B tests across two revisions of the same service.
Cloud Run Jobs — Jobs do not have HTTP traffic; use task execution and retry controls instead.
Service deployment configuration — see cloud-run-deployment for gcloud flags and Terraform.
Autoscaling and concurrency tuning — see cloud-run-autoscaling.

rule.statement: Every production deployment MUST use --no-traffic --tag=green (or a semantically versioned tag) to deploy the new revision without receiving traffic. The tagged URL (https://green---service-hash.run.app) MUST be tested before shifting traffic. Tags persist across deployments and provide stable preview URLs for the lifetime of the tagged revision.
rule.rationale: Deploying with --no-traffic means zero production impact if the new revision has a bug. The tagged URL gives a dedicated test surface that is identical to production (same container, same env, same secrets) but isolated from live traffic. Without tags, the only way to test a new revision is to send it live traffic.

example.title: Deploy without traffic and test via tag URL
example.code:
# Step 1: Deploy new revision with tag, no traffic gcloud run deploy my-app \ --image=us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/my-repo/my-app:v2 \ --region=us-central1 \ --no-traffic \ --tag=green
# Step 2: Test via tagged URL curl https://green---my-app-xxxxx-uc.a.run.app/health
# Step 3: Shift 100% traffic to green gcloud run services update-traffic my-app \ --region=us-central1 \ --to-tags=green=100

antipattern.description: Deploying directly to production with 100% traffic without a staging or tagged-revision test step.
antipattern.reason: A bad deployment immediately affects all users. The time to detect and rollback is measured in minutes (monitoring alert + human response), during which errors accumulate. Tagged deployments reduce impact to zero until validated.

rule.statement: Blue-green deployments on Cloud Run: deploy the new revision (green) with --no-traffic --tag=green, validate health at the tagged URL, then shift 100% traffic in one atomic update-traffic call. Keep the previous revision available without deleting it for at least one deployment cycle — this enables instant rollback.
rule.rationale: The one-atomic-shift approach means there is no period where traffic splits between old and new. For services where mixed-version requests would cause data inconsistency (schema migrations, API contract changes), blue-green is safer than canary. The old revision stays warm via min-instances or traffic routing and can absorb 100% traffic again within seconds via --to-revisions.

example.title: Full blue-green deployment workflow
example.code:
# 1. Deploy green revision without traffic gcloud run deploy my-app \ --image=us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/my-repo/my-app:v2.0.0 \ --region=us-central1 \ --no-traffic \ --tag=green
# 2. Test green revision via tag URL curl https://green---my-app-xxxxx-uc.a.run.app curl https://green---my-app-xxxxx-uc.a.run.app/health
# 3. Shift 100% traffic to green (atomic) gcloud run services update-traffic my-app \ --region=us-central1 \ --to-tags=green=100
# 4. (Optional) Rollback to previous if issues found gcloud run services update-traffic my-app \ --region=us-central1 \ --to-revisions=my-app-00005-abc=100

rule.statement: Canary deployments: deploy new revision with a canary tag, route a small percentage of traffic (5-10%), monitor error rate and latency for at least 10 minutes, then increment traffic in stages (5% → 25% → 50% → 100%). Automate rollback if error rate exceeds a threshold. Do NOT run canary for longer than 24 hours — operational complexity increases with duration.
rule.rationale: A 5% canary limits blast radius to 5% of users. Cloud Run's traffic splitting is weighted by revision percentage, not by sticky session, so each request is independently routed. This gives a statistically significant sample without session affinity complexity. Monitoring error rate and p99 latency during each stage provides the signal to proceed or rollback.

example.title: Canary deployment with gradual traffic shift
example.code:
# Deploy canary revision gcloud run deploy my-app \ --image=us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/my-repo/my-app:v2 \ --region=us-central1 \ --tag=canary
# Route 5% of traffic to canary gcloud run services update-traffic my-app \ --region=us-central1 \ --to-tags=canary=5
# Monitor for 10+ minutes, then increase to 25% gcloud run services update-traffic my-app \ --region=us-central1 \ --to-tags=canary=25
# Continue: 50% gcloud run services update-traffic my-app \ --region=us-central1 \ --to-tags=canary=50
# Full rollout gcloud run services update-traffic my-app \ --region=us-central1 \ --to-latest

example.title: Rollback canary: revert to stable revision
example.code:
# List revisions to find the stable one gcloud run revisions list --service=my-app --region=us-central1
# Roll back to a named revision (100% traffic) gcloud run services update-traffic my-app \ --region=us-central1 \ --to-revisions=my-app-00005-abc=100

example.title: Revision lifecycle management
example.code:
# List all revisions with traffic allocation gcloud run revisions list --service=my-app --region=us-central1
# Delete old revisions (except those serving traffic) gcloud run revisions list --service=my-app --region=us-central1 \ --format="value(name)" | tail -n +6 | \ xargs -I {} gcloud run revisions delete {} --region=us-central1 --quiet
example.note: Revisions accumulate over time. Cloud Run retains all revisions; delete old ones that are not serving traffic to reduce noise in the revision list. Never delete a revision serving any % of traffic.

antipattern.description: Using --to-latest in canary rollout instead of --to-tags=canary=N.
antipattern.reason: --to-latest routes 100% traffic to the newest revision immediately, bypassing canary validation. Always use --to-tags with explicit percentages during staged rollouts.

example.title: Cloud Build pipeline with tagged blue-green deploy
example.code:
steps: - name: 'python:3.12' entrypoint: bash args: - '-c' - | pip install -r requirements.txt pytest tests/
- name: 'gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker' args: - 'build' - '-t' - '${_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/${_REPO}/${_SERVICE}:${SHORT_SHA}' - '.'
- name: 'gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker' args: - 'push' - '${_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/${_REPO}/${_SERVICE}:${SHORT_SHA}'
# Deploy without traffic (green) - name: 'gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk' entrypoint: gcloud args: - 'run' - 'deploy' - '${_SERVICE}' - '--image=${_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/${_REPO}/${_SERVICE}:${SHORT_SHA}' - '--region=${_REGION}' - '--no-traffic' - '--tag=green'
# Shift 100% traffic to green after smoke test - name: 'gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk' entrypoint: gcloud args: - 'run' - 'services' - 'update-traffic' - '${_SERVICE}' - '--region=${_REGION}' - '--to-tags=green=100'
substitutions: _REGION: us-central1 _REPO: my-repo _SERVICE: my-service

checklist.check: Tag assigned to new revision before traffic shift.
No-traffic deployment used for new revisions in production.
New revision tested via tagged URL before receiving live traffic.
Traffic split configured (canary percentage set explicitly).
Rollback plan documented (specific revision name noted).
Monitoring alerts configured for error rate and latency during rollout.
Old revision retained (not deleted) until new revision is stable.
Final traffic shift verified (100% to new revision confirmed).
